source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure mounting of udf filesystems is disabled
description: The udf filesystem type is the universal disk format used to implement ISO/IEC 13346 and ECMA-167 specifications. This is an open vendor filesystem type for data storage on a broad range of media. This filesystem type is necessary to support writing DVDs and newer optical disc formats.
rationale: Removing support for unneeded filesystem types reduces the local attack surface of the system. If this filesystem type is not needed, disable it.
audit: Run the following commands and verify the output is as indicated: # modprobe -n -v udf install /bin/true # lsmod | grep udf <No output>
remediation: Edit or create a file in the /etc/modprobe.d/ directory ending in .conf Example: vim /etc/modprobe.d/udf.conf and add the following line: install udf /bin/true Run the following command to unload the udf module: # rmmod udf
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires OS-level inspection (kernel module configuration and loaded modules) that is not accessible via any GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native service. The only way to verify compliance is by directly connecting to each instance and running the specified commands, as GCP does not expose kernel module status or configuration file contents through its APIs.
manual_summary: 1. **Connect to the Container-Optimized OS Instance:** - Use SSH to connect to each relevant GCP VM instance: `gcloud compute ssh INSTANCE_NAME --zone ZONE` 2. **Check UDF Filesystem Module Configuration:** - Run: `modprobe -n -v udf` - Confirm the output is: `install /bin/true` 3. **Check if UDF Module is Loaded:** - Run: `lsmod | grep udf` - Confirm there is no output (the module is not loaded) 4. **Validate Configuration File:** - Open `/etc/modprobe.d/udf.conf` and verify it contains: `install udf /bin/true` 5. **Document Compliance:** - Record the results for each instance checked, confirming all above conditions are met.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure /tmp is configured
description: The /tmp directory is a world-writable directory used for temporary storage by all users and some applications.
rationale: Making /tmp its own file system allows an administrator to set the noexec option on the mount, making /tmp useless for an attacker to install executable code. It would also prevent an attacker from establishing a hardlink to a system setuid program and wait for it to be updated. Once the program was updated, the hardlink would be broken and the attacker would have his own copy of the program. If the program happened to have a security vulnerability, the attacker could continue to exploit the known flaw. This can be accomplished by either mounting tmpfs to /tmp, or creating a separate partition for /tmp. Impact: Since the /tmp directory is intended to be world-writable, there is a risk of resource exhaustion if it is not bound to a separate partition. Running out of /tmp space is a problem regardless of what kind of filesystem lies under it, but in a default installation a disk-based /tmp will essentially have the whole disk available, as it only creates a single / partition. On the other hand, a RAM-based /tmp as with tmpfs will almost certainly be much smaller, which can lead to applications filling up the filesystem much more easily. /tmp utilizing tmpfs can be resized using the size={size} parameter on the Options line on the tmp.mount file
audit: Run the following command and verify output shows /tmp is mounted: # mount | grep -E '\s/tmp\s' tmpfs on /tmp type tmpfs (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime) Run the following command and verify that output shows static # systemctl is-enabled tmp.mount static
remediation: Run the following commands to configure mount options systemctl edit tmp.mount Edit the file to define the options that needs to be set. For example: [Mount] Options= Options=mode=1777,strictatime,noexec,nodev,nosuid Restart the tmp.mount systemctl daemon-reload # might be optional systemctl restart tmp.mount /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot. References: 1. AJ Lewis, "LVM HOWTO", http://tldp.org/HOWTO/LVM-HOWTO/ 2. https://www.freedesktop.org/wiki/Software/systemd/APIFileSystems/
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires verification of the `/tmp` mount configuration inside the guest OS of a Container-Optimized OS instance. GCP APIs, Google Cloud SDK, and GCP Config do not expose runtime mount options or systemd unit states within the guest OS. These details are only accessible via direct OS-level commands (e.g., `mount`, `systemctl`) after SSH access. Therefore, automation via GCP-native tools is not possible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Instance:** - Use the GCP Console or `gcloud compute ssh` to access the Container-Optimized OS instance. 2. **Verify /tmp Mount:** - Run: `mount | grep -E '\s/tmp\s'` - Confirm that `/tmp` is mounted as a separate `tmpfs` filesystem with the options: `rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime`. 3. **Check tmp.mount Status:** - Run: `systemctl is-enabled tmp.mount` - Ensure the output is `static`. 4. **Review Mount Options:** - Optionally, run `systemctl cat tmp.mount` or `systemctl edit tmp.mount` to inspect that the `Options=` line includes `mode=1777,strictatime,noexec,nodev,nosuid`. 5. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - `/tmp` must be a separate mount point with the above options set for security. 6. **Confirm Compliance:** - Only if all the above checks pass, the instance is compliant.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure nodev option set on /tmp partition
description: The nodev mount option specifies that the filesystem cannot contain special devices.
rationale: Since the /tmp filesystem is not intended to support devices, set this option to ensure that users cannot attempt to create block or character special devices in /tmp .
audit: Verify that the nodev option is set if a /tmp partition exists Run the following command and verify that nothing is returned: # mount | grep -E '\s/tmp\s' | grep -v nodev
remediation: Run the following command to remount /tmp : # mount -o remount,nodev /tmp /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires verification of the `nodev` mount option on the `/tmp` partition within the guest OS of a Container-Optimized OS (COS) VM. There is no GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native tool that can directly query or enforce the mount options of a running VM's filesystems. This check must be performed from within the instance itself, typically via SSH, as the required information is not exposed to GCP management layers.
manual_summary: 1. **Identify the Target Instance:** - In the Google Cloud Console, navigate to Compute Engine > VM instances. - Locate the VM running Container-Optimized OS that you wish to audit. 2. **Access the Instance:** - SSH into the instance using the Cloud Console or via command line: ```bash gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone=<ZONE> ``` 3. **Verify Mount Options:** - Run the following command inside the instance: ```bash mount | grep -E '\s/tmp\s' | grep -v nodev ``` - **What to verify:** The command should return nothing. If it returns output, the `/tmp` partition is missing the `nodev` option and is non-compliant. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The `/tmp` partition must have the `nodev` option set in its mount options. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document the command output as evidence. - If remediation is needed, remount `/tmp` with the `nodev` option: ```bash sudo mount -o remount,nodev /tmp ``` - Note: This change is not persistent across reboots on COS and must be re-applied after every boot.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure nosuid option set on /tmp partition
description: The nosuid mount option specifies that the filesystem cannot contain setuid files.
rationale: Since the /tmp filesystem is only intended for temporary file storage, set this option to ensure that users cannot create setuid files in /tmp .
audit: Verify that the nosuid option is set if a /tmp partition exists Run the following command and verify that nothing is returned: # mount | grep -E '\s/tmp\s' | grep -v nosuid
remediation: Run the following command to remount /tmp : # mount -o remount,nosuid /tmp /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the nosuid mount option on the /tmp partition requires direct inspection within the guest OS of the VM. GCP APIs, including the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), do not expose live mount options for filesystems inside running instances. There is no GCP-native method to retrieve this information without SSH access to the instance, making automation via GCP APIs impossible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target GCP VM instance running Container-Optimized OS using the GCP Console or the command: `gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone=<ZONE>` 2. Run the following command to check the mount options for the /tmp partition: `mount | grep -E '\s/tmp\s' | grep -v nosuid` 3. Review the output: - If nothing is returned, the nosuid option is set and the instance is compliant. - If any output is returned, the nosuid option is missing and remediation is required. 4. Remediate if necessary by running: `sudo mount -o remount,nosuid /tmp` (Note: This change is not persistent across reboots on Container-Optimized OS.) 5. Document the result for compliance records.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Disable Automounting
description: autofs allows automatic mounting of devices, typically including CD/DVDs and USB drives.
rationale: With automounting enabled anyone with physical access could attach a USB drive or disc and have its contents available in system even if they lacked permissions to mount it themselves. Impact: The use of portable hard drives is very common for workstation users. If your organization allows the use of portable storage or media on workstations and physical access controls to workstations is considered adequate there is little value add in turning off automounting.
audit: # systemctl is-enabled autofs disabled Verify result is not "enabled".
remediation: Run the following command to disable autofs: # systemctl disable autofs Additional Information: Additional methods of disabling a service exist. Consult your distribution documentation for appropriate methods. This control should align with the tolerance of the use of portable drives and optical media in the organization. On a server requiring an admin to manually mount media can be part of defense-in-depth to reduce the risk of unapproved software or information being introduced or proprietary software or information being exfiltrated. If admins commonly use flash drives and Server access has sufficient physical controls, requiring manual mounting may not increase security.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the status of the autofs service (for automounting) is an OS-level configuration inside each VM instance and is not exposed or manageable via any GCP API, gcloud command, or native GCP service. There is no GCP-native method to directly check or enforce the autofs service state without SSH access to the instance, making automation via GCP APIs impossible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each relevant VM instance running Container Optimized OS. 2. Run the command: `systemctl is-enabled autofs` 3. Verify that the output is NOT "enabled". If it is "enabled", the instance is non-compliant. 4. If remediation is needed, run: `sudo systemctl disable autofs` 5. Re-run `systemctl is-enabled autofs` to confirm the service is now disabled. 6. Document the results for compliance records.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure dm-verity is enabled
description: device-mapper-verity (dm-verity) kernel feature feature provides transparent integrity checking of block devices using a cryptographic digest provided by the kernel crypto API. When a dm-verity device is configured, it is expected that the caller has been authenticated in some way (cryptographic signatures, etc). After instantiation, all hashes will be verified on-demand during disk access. If they cannot be verified up to the root node of the tree, the root hash, then the I/O will fail. This should detect tampering with any data on the device and the hash data.
rationale: The Container-Optimized OS root filesystem is always mounted as read-only. Additionally, its checksum is computed at build time and verified by the kernel on each boot. This mechanism prevents against attackers from "owning" the machine through permanent local changes.
audit: Verify dm-verity is enabled in kernel config with the following command: # zcat /proc/config.gz | grep CONFIG_DM_VERITY CONFIG_DM_VERITY=y
remediation: An OS image update that has the dm-verity enabled kernel is required. References: 1. AIDE stable manual: http://aide.sourceforge.net/stable/manual.html Additional Information: The prelinking feature can interfere with AIDE because it alters binaries to speed up their start up times. Run prelink -ua to restore the binaries to their prelinked state, thus avoiding false positives from AIDE.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying whether dm-verity is enabled requires direct inspection of the kernel configuration on the running Container-Optimized OS instance. There are no GCP APIs, gcloud commands, or other automated mechanisms that expose the kernel compile-time options or the dm-verity status for Compute Engine instances. This check must be performed manually via SSH access to the instance.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Compute Engine Instance:** - Use the Google Cloud Console or terminal to SSH into the target Container-Optimized OS instance. - Example command: `gcloud compute ssh [INSTANCE_NAME] --zone=[ZONE] --project=[PROJECT_ID]` 2. **Check Kernel Configuration for dm-verity:** - Run the following command on the instance: `zcat /proc/config.gz | grep CONFIG_DM_VERITY` 3. **Verify Output:** - Confirm that the output includes `CONFIG_DM_VERITY=y`. - This indicates that dm-verity is enabled in the kernel configuration. 4. **Determine Compliance:** - If `CONFIG_DM_VERITY=y` is present, the instance is compliant. - If not, remediation (such as updating to a compliant OS image) is required.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure authentication required for single user mode
description: Single user mode (rescue mode) is used for recovery when the system detects an issue during boot or by manual selection from the bootloader.
rationale: Requiring authentication in single user mode (rescue mode) prevents an unauthorized user from rebooting the system into single user to gain root privileges without credentials.
audit: Run the following commands and verify that /sbin/sulogin or /usr/sbin/sulogin is used as shown: # grep /systemd-sulogin-shell /usr/lib/systemd/system/rescue.service ExecStart=-/usr/lib/systemd/systemd-sulogin-shell rescue # grep /systemd-sulogin-shell /usr/lib/systemd/system/emergency.service ExecStart=-/usr/lib/systemd/systemd-sulogin-shell emergency
remediation: Rootfs is read-only file system. Therefore, update to an OS image which requires single user mode authentication.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying authentication for single user mode on Google Container-Optimized OS requires direct inspection of OS-level configuration files (`/usr/lib/systemd/system/rescue.service` and `/usr/lib/systemd/system/emergency.service`). GCP APIs, Cloud SDK, and OS Config do not provide access to arbitrary in-guest file contents for audit purposes. Therefore, SSH-based manual inspection is the only feasible approach.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Instance:** Use the GCP Console or the following command to access the target Container-Optimized OS instance: ```bash gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> ``` 2. **Check Rescue Service Configuration:** Run the following command to verify the rescue service uses sulogin for authentication: ```bash grep '/systemd-sulogin-shell' /usr/lib/systemd/system/rescue.service ``` - Confirm that the output includes: `ExecStart=-/usr/lib/systemd/systemd-sulogin-shell rescue` 3. **Check Emergency Service Configuration:** Run the following command to verify the emergency service uses sulogin for authentication: ```bash grep '/systemd-sulogin-shell' /usr/lib/systemd/system/emergency.service ``` - Confirm that the output includes: `ExecStart=-/usr/lib/systemd/systemd-sulogin-shell emergency` 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** Both files must reference `systemd-sulogin-shell` to ensure authentication is required in single user mode. 5. **Why Automation Isn't Feasible:** GCP APIs, SDKs, and OS Config do not expose or allow querying of arbitrary guest OS file contents; only manual inspection via SSH can confirm the required configuration.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure core dumps are restricted
description: A core dump is the memory of an executable program. It is generally used to determine why a program aborted. It can also be used to glean confidential information from a core file. The system provides the ability to set a soft limit for core dumps, but this can be overridden by the user.
rationale: Setting a hard limit on core dumps prevents users from overriding the soft variable. If core dumps are required, consider setting limits for user groups (see limits.conf(5) ). In addition, setting the fs.suid_dumpable variable to 0 will prevent setuid programs from dumping core.
audit: Run the following commands and verify output matches: # grep "hard core" /etc/security/limits.conf /etc/security/limits.d/* * hard core 0 # sysctl fs.suid_dumpable fs.suid_dumpable = 0 Run the following commands to check if systemd-coredump is installed: # systemctl is-enabled systemd-coredump@.service # systemctl is-enabled systemd-coredump.socket if static is returned systemd-coredump is installed
remediation: Add the following line to /etc/security/limits.conf or a /etc/security/limits.d/* file: * hard core 0 Run the following command to set the active kernel parameter: # sysctl -w fs.suid_dumpable=0 If systemd-coredump@ is installed: edit /etc/systemd/coredump.conf and add/modify the following lines: Storage=none ProcessSizeMax=0  Run the command: systemctl daemon-reload /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires inspection of OS-level configuration files and kernel parameters inside the guest operating system (Container-Optimized OS). These settings—such as `/etc/security/limits.conf`, `/etc/security/limits.d/*`, and the `fs.suid_dumpable` kernel parameter—are not exposed via any GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native compliance tooling. Therefore, automation through GCP APIs is not technically possible.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Instance:** - Use the GCP Console or `gcloud compute ssh` to connect to the target VM running Container-Optimized OS. 2. **Check Core Dump Limits:** - Run: `grep "hard core" /etc/security/limits.conf /etc/security/limits.d/*` - Confirm the output contains: `* hard core 0` - This ensures a hard limit is set for all users. 3. **Verify Kernel Parameter:** - Run: `sysctl fs.suid_dumpable` - Confirm the output is: `fs.suid_dumpable = 0` - This prevents setuid programs from dumping core. 4. **Check for systemd-coredump:** - Run: `systemctl is-enabled systemd-coredump@.service` `systemctl is-enabled systemd-coredump.socket` - If either returns `static`, systemd-coredump is installed. - If installed, check `/etc/systemd/coredump.conf` for: ``` Storage=none ProcessSizeMax=0 ``` - Run: `systemctl daemon-reload` - This ensures core dumps are not stored. 5. **Document Results:** - Record command outputs and configuration file contents as evidence of compliance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure XD/NX support is enabled
description: The NX bit (no-execute) is a technology used in CPUs to segregate areas of memory for use by either storage of processor instructions or for storage of data. An operating system with support for the NX bit may mark certain areas of memory as non- executable. The processor will then refuse to execute any code residing in these areas of memory. This ability can help prevent exploitation of buffer overflow vulnerabilities and should be activated whenever possible. Recent processors in the x86 family support the ability to prevent code execution on a per memory page basis. On AMD processors, this ability is called No Execute (NX), on Intel processors it is called Execute Disable (XD) and on ARM processors it is called Execute Never (XN). Extra steps must be taken to ensure that this protection is enabled, particularly on 32-bit x86 systems. Other processors, such as Itanium and POWER, have included such support since inception and the standard kernel for those platforms supports the feature. Starting from ARMv6, the XN bit is supported by default and the kernel cannot disable it. For this reason, this recommend is not applicable for Container-Optimized OS ARM images.
rationale: Enabling any feature that can protect against buffer overflow attacks enhances the security of the system.
audit: Run the following command and verify your kernel has identified and activated NX/XD protection. # journalctl | grep 'protection: active' kernel: NX (Execute Disable) protection: active OR on systems without journalctl [[ -n $(grep noexec[0-9]*=off /proc/cmdline) || -z $(grep -E -i ' (pae|nx) ' /proc/cpuinfo) || -n $(grep '\sNX\s.*\sprotection:\s' /var/log/dmesg | grep - v active) ]] && echo "NX Protection is not active" Nothing should be returned
remediation: On 32 bit systems install a kernel with PAE support, no installation is required on 64 bit systems: If necessary configure your bootloader to load the new kernel and reboot the system. You may need to enable NX or XD support in your bios. Additional Information: Ensure your system supports the XD or NX bit and has PAE support before implementing this recommendation as this may prevent it from booting if these are not supported by your hardware.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying NX/XD support requires inspection of OS-level kernel and CPU flags within the guest VM, which is not exposed via any GCP API, Google Cloud SDK, or CLI. GCP does not provide a mechanism to remotely query or validate these low-level runtime settings from outside the VM instance. Therefore, compliance must be checked by directly accessing the instance and running the specified commands.
manual_summary: 1. Identify all Compute Engine VM instances running Container-Optimized OS in your GCP project. 2. SSH into each target VM instance using either the GCP Console (click "SSH" next to the instance) or the command: ```bash gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone <ZONE> ``` 3. Once logged in, run the following command to check for NX protection: ```bash journalctl | grep 'protection: active' ``` - If nothing is returned, proceed with the alternative check: ```bash [[ -n $(grep noexec[0-9]*=off /proc/cmdline) || -z $(grep -E -i ' (pae|nx) ' /proc/cpuinfo) || -n $(grep '\sNX\s.*\sprotection:\s' /var/log/dmesg | grep -v active) ]] && echo "NX Protection is not active" ``` 4. Confirm that NX (Execute Disable) protection is reported as "active" and that no output indicates "NX Protection is not active." 5. Document the results for each instance as evidence of compliance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure address space layout randomization (ASLR) is enabled
description: Address space layout randomization (ASLR) is an exploit mitigation technique which randomly arranges the address space of key data areas of a process.
rationale: Randomly placing virtual memory regions will make it difficult to write memory page exploits as the memory placement will be consistently shifting.
audit: Run the following command and verify output matches: # sysctl kernel.randomize_va_space kernel.randomize_va_space = 2 # grep "kernel\.randomize_va_space" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/* kernel.randomize_va_space = 2
remediation: Set the following parameter in /etc/sysctl.conf or a /etc/sysctl.d/* file: kernel.randomize_va_space = 2 Run the following command to set the active kernel parameter: # sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that there is no GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native tool that can directly query or validate the kernel parameter `kernel.randomize_va_space` inside a running VM instance. This setting is only accessible from within the guest OS via SSH or similar direct access. GCP OS Config and Inventory do not expose sysctl kernel parameters by default, and there is no supported automated method to check this control at scale using GCP-native tools.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Container Optimized OS (COS) instance you wish to audit. 2. Run the following command to check the current ASLR setting: ```bash sysctl kernel.randomize_va_space ``` - Confirm the output is: `kernel.randomize_va_space = 2` 3. Verify persistent configuration by running: ```bash grep "kernel\.randomize_va_space" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/* ``` - Confirm the output includes: `kernel.randomize_va_space = 2` 4. Both the runtime and persistent settings must show `2` for compliance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure message of the day is configured properly
description: The contents of the /etc/motd file are displayed to users after login and function as a message of the day for authenticated users. Unix-based systems have typically displayed information about the OS release and patch level upon logging in to the system. This information can be useful to developers who are developing software for a particular OS platform. If mingetty(8) supports the following options, they display operating system information: \m - machine architecture \r - operating system release \s - operating system name \v - operating system version
rationale: Warning messages inform users who are attempting to login to the system of their legal status regarding the system and must include the name of the organization that owns the system and any monitoring policies that are in place. Displaying OS and patch level information in login banners also has the side effect of providing detailed system information to attackers attempting to target specific exploits of a system. Authorized users can easily get this information by running the " uname -a " command once they have logged in.
audit: Run the following command and verify that the contents match site policy: # cat /etc/motd Run the following command and verify no results are returned: # grep -E -i "(\\v|\\r|\\m|\\s|$(grep '^ID=' /etc/os-release | cut -d= -f2 | sed -e 's/"//g'))" /etc/motd
remediation: Edit the /etc/motd file with the appropriate contents according to your site policy, remove any instances of \m , \r , \s , \v or references to the OS platform /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, the steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the contents of `/etc/motd` on Container-Optimized OS instances are not accessible or manageable via any GCP API, gcloud CLI, or GCP-native configuration tools. The control requires direct inspection of a file inside the guest OS, which can only be performed by logging into the instance. No GCP-native automation or API can retrieve or validate the `/etc/motd` file contents, making manual inspection the only feasible approach.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Container-Optimized OS instance using the GCP Console or the following command: ``` gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone <ZONE> ``` 2. Display the contents of the `/etc/motd` file: ``` cat /etc/motd ``` 3. Verify that the message complies with your organization's policy and does NOT contain OS or patch level information (such as `\m`, `\r`, `\s`, `\v`, or the OS name from `/etc/os-release`). 4. Run the following command to check for unauthorized information: ``` grep -E -i "(\\v|\\r|\\m|\\s|$(grep '^ID=' /etc/os-release | cut -d= -f2 | sed -e 's/\"//g'))" /etc/motd ``` Ensure that this command returns no results. 5. If non-compliant, edit `/etc/motd` to remove any prohibited information and ensure it meets site policy.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure local login warning banner is configured properly
description: The contents of the /etc/issue file are displayed to users prior to login for local terminals. Unix-based systems have typically displayed information about the OS release and patch level upon logging in to the system. This information can be useful to developers who are developing software for a particular OS platform. If mingetty(8) supports the following options, they display operating system information: \m - machine architecture \r - operating system release \s - operating system name \v - operating system version - or the operating system's name
rationale: Warning messages inform users who are attempting to login to the system of their legal status regarding the system and must include the name of the organization that owns the system and any monitoring policies that are in place. Displaying OS and patch level information in login banners also has the side effect of providing detailed system information to attackers attempting to target specific exploits of a system. Authorized users can easily get this information by running the " uname -a " command once they have logged in.
audit: Run the following command and verify that the contents match site policy: # cat /etc/issue Run the following command and verify no results are returned: # grep -E -i "(\\v|\\r|\\m|\\s|$(grep '^ID=' /etc/os-release | cut -d= -f2 | sed -e 's/"//g'))" /etc/issue
remediation: Edit the /etc/issue file with the appropriate contents according to your site policy, remove any instances of \m , \r , \s , \v or references to the OS platform # echo "Authorized uses only. All activity may be monitored and reported." > /etc/issue /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, the steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires direct inspection of the `/etc/issue` file on the guest OS of a Container-Optimized OS instance. There is no GCP API, SDK, or CLI method that exposes the contents of this file for audit purposes. Accessing or validating this file's content requires SSH access to the instance, which is inherently a manual process outside the scope of GCP-native automation.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target Container-Optimized OS instance using `gcloud compute ssh <instance-name> --zone <zone>`. 2. Run `cat /etc/issue` to display the current login banner. 3. Verify that the banner message matches your organization's policy and does not contain any of the following placeholders: `\m`, `\r`, `\s`, `\v`, or any OS/platform-specific information. 4. Run `grep -E -i "(\\v|\\r|\\m|\\s|$(grep '^ID=' /etc/os-release | cut -d= -f2 | sed -e 's/"//g'))" /etc/issue` to confirm that no OS information is present. 5. Ensure the banner contains a legal or informational message as required by your organization (e.g., "Authorized uses only. All activity may be monitored and reported."). 6. Document the findings, including the exact contents of `/etc/issue` and the results of the grep command.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure permissions on /etc/motd are configured
description: The contents of the /etc/motd file are displayed to users after login and function as a message of the day for authenticated users.
rationale: If the /etc/motd file does not have the correct ownership it could be modified by unauthorized users with incorrect or misleading information.
audit: Run the following command and verify Uid and Gid are both 0/root and Access is 644 : # stat /etc/motd Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
remediation: Run the following commands to set permissions on /etc/motd : # chown root:root /etc/motd # chmod 644 /etc/motd /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, the steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the ownership and permissions of the `/etc/motd` file on a VM running Container-Optimized OS requires direct OS-level inspection, which is not possible via GCP APIs, gcloud, or any GCP-native automation tools. GCP APIs do not provide visibility into guest OS file permissions or ownership, making manual SSH access and command execution the only viable method.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the Target VM Instance:** - Use SSH to connect to the GCP VM instance running Container-Optimized OS where the `/etc/motd` file resides. 2. **Verify File Ownership and Permissions:** - Run the following command: ``` stat /etc/motd ``` - Confirm that: - Uid is `0` (root) - Gid is `0` (root) - Access permissions are `644` (`-rw-r--r--`) 3. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The `/etc/motd` file must be owned by the root user and group, with permissions set to `644` (read/write for owner, read-only for group and others). 4. **Remediate if Necessary:** - If the file is not compliant, run: ``` chown root:root /etc/motd chmod 644 /etc/motd ``` - Note: Because `/etc` is stateless on Container-Optimized OS, these checks and corrections must be performed after every boot. 5. **Document Compliance:** - Record the output of the `stat /etc/motd` command and any remediation actions taken for audit purposes.
