source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure root is the only UID 0 account
description: Any account with UID 0 has superuser privileges on the system.
rationale: This access must be limited to only the default root account and only from the system console. Administrative access must be through an unprivileged account using an approved mechanism as noted in Item 5.6 Ensure access to the su command is restricted.
audit: Run the following command and verify that only "root" is returned: # awk -F: '($3 == 0) { print $1 }' /etc/passwd root
remediation: Remove any users other than root with UID 0 or assign them a new UID if appropriate.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires inspection of the `/etc/passwd` file within the guest OS to enumerate accounts with UID 0. GCP APIs, Google Cloud SDK, and related tools do not provide access to OS-level user account information. Therefore, compliance can only be verified by manually accessing each instance and running the specified command.
manual_summary: 1. Use SSH to connect to each GCP instance running Container-Optimized OS: `gcloud compute ssh [INSTANCE_NAME] --zone [ZONE]` 2. Run the following command to list all accounts with UID 0: `awk -F: '($3 == 0) { print $1 }' /etc/passwd` 3. Verify that the output is only "root". - If any other usernames appear, the instance is non-compliant. 4. Document any deviations and remediate by removing or reassigning UIDs for non-root accounts with UID 0. 5. Record compliance status for each instance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure root PATH Integrity
description: The root user can execute any command on the system and could be fooled into executing programs unintentionally if the PATH is not set correctly.
rationale: Including the current working directory (.) or other writable directory in root 's executable path makes it likely that an attacker can gain superuser access by forcing an administrator operating as root to execute a Trojan horse program.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash PATH="$(sudo -Hiu root env | grep '^PATH' | cut -d= -f2)" echo "$PATH" | grep -q "::" && echo "Empty directory in PATH (::)" echo "$PATH" | grep -q ":$" && echo "Trailing : in PATH" for dir in $(echo "$PATH" | tr ":" " "); do if [ -d "$dir" ]; then ls -ldH "$dir" | awk 'substr($1,6,1) != "-" {print $9, "is group writable"} substr($1,9,1) != "-" {print $9, "is world writable"} $3 != "root" {print $9, "is not owned by root"}' else base_dir=$(echo "$dir" | cut -d "/" -f2) # Ignore if directory is on read-only partition rw=$(findmnt -T "/$base_dir" | sed '1d' | grep -v "\sro,") if [ -n "$rw" ]; then echo "$dir is not a directory" fi fi done
remediation: Correct or justify any items discovered in the Audit step.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires direct inspection of the root user's PATH environment variable and the permissions/ownership of directories referenced therein. These are OS-level attributes not exposed or accessible via any GCP API, Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), or GCP-native service. The check must be performed on the running instance itself, requiring SSH access and execution of the provided script. No GCP-native automation is possible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. In the GCP Console, navigate to Compute Engine > VM instances and identify the instance running Google Container-Optimized OS to be audited. 2. SSH into the instance as a user with sudo privileges. 3. Switch to the root user (if not already root) by running: `sudo -i` 4. Copy and paste the provided audit script into the shell and execute it: ```bash PATH="$(sudo -Hiu root env | grep '^PATH' | cut -d= -f2)" echo "$PATH" | grep -q "::" && echo "Empty directory in PATH (::)" echo "$PATH" | grep -q ":$" && echo "Trailing : in PATH" for dir in $(echo "$PATH" | tr ":" " "); do if [ -d "$dir" ]; then ls -ldH "$dir" | awk 'substr($1,6,1) != "-" {print $9, "is group writable"} substr($1,9,1) != "-" {print $9, "is world writable"} $3 != "root" {print $9, "is not owned by root"}' fi done ``` 5. Review the script output: - There should be no "Empty directory in PATH (::)", "Trailing : in PATH", or any directory flagged as group/world writable or not owned by root. 6. If any issues are reported, remediate by correcting the PATH variable or directory permissions/ownership as appropriate. 7. Document the results and remediation actions (if any) for compliance records.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure all users' home directories exist
description: Users can be defined in /etc/passwd without a home directory or with a home directory that does not actually exist.
rationale: If the user's home directory does not exist or is unassigned, the user will be placed in "/" and will not be able to write any files or have local environment variables set.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read -r user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." fi done
remediation: If any users' home directories do not exist, create them and make sure the respective user owns the directory. Users without an assigned home directory should be removed or assigned a home directory as appropriate. Additional Information: The audit script checks all users UID 500 and above except nfsnobody. Some distributions split at UID 1000 instead, consult your documentation and/or the UID_MIN setting in /etc/login.defs to determine which is appropriate for you. On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires inspection of the guest OS file system to verify the existence of users' home directories, which is not possible via GCP APIs, Google Cloud SDK, or GCP CLI. GCP does not expose internal OS-level file system details (such as /etc/passwd or home directory existence) through any of its cloud-native APIs or management tools. Therefore, the only feasible method is to manually access the VM instance and run the provided script.
manual_summary: 1. **Identify Target Instances:** - In the GCP Console, go to Compute Engine > VM instances. - Filter or identify instances running Container Optimized OS that require auditing. 2. **Access the Instance:** - SSH into each target instance using either the SSH button in the GCP Console or the following command: ```bash gcloud compute ssh <instance-name> --zone=<instance-zone> ``` 3. **Run the Audit Script:** - On the instance, execute the following script to check for users whose home directories do not exist: ```bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'$(which nologin)'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read -r user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." fi done ``` 4. **Verify Output:** - Confirm that the script returns no output. If any lines are returned, those users do not have valid home directories and must be remediated. 5. **Document Results:** - Record the outcome for compliance evidence.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure users' home directories permissions are 750 or more restrictive
description: While the system administrator can establish secure permissions for users' home directories, the users can easily override these.
rationale: Group or world-writable user home directories may enable malicious users to steal or modify other users' data or to gain another user's system privileges.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else dirperm=$(ls -ld $dir | cut -f1 -d" ") if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c6) != "-" ]; then echo "Group Write permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c8) != "-" ]; then echo "Other Read permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c9) != "-" ]; then echo "Other Write permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c10) != "-" ]; then echo "Other Execute permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi fi done
remediation: Making global modifications to user home directories without alerting the user community can result in unexpected outages and unhappy users. Therefore, it is recommended that a monitoring policy be established to report user file permissions and determine the action to be taken in accordance with site policy. Additional Information: On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires inspection of OS-level file permissions within Compute Engine instances, which is not accessible via any GCP-native APIs, SDKs, or CLI tools. GCP does not expose guest OS file system permissions through its management interfaces; this check requires privileged access to the VM's operating system, typically via SSH, to execute the provided script.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each GCP Compute Engine instance running Container-Optimized OS. 2. Run the following script on the instance to check user home directory permissions: ```bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'$(which nologin)'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else dirperm=$(ls -ld $dir | cut -f1 -d" ") if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c6) != "-" ]; then echo "Group Write permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c8) != "-" ]; then echo "Other Read permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c9) != "-" ]; then echo "Other Write permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi if [ $(echo $dirperm | cut -c10) != "-" ]; then echo "Other Execute permission set on the home directory ($dir) of user $user" fi fi done ``` 3. Review the script output. No output indicates compliance; any output indicates non-compliance. 4. Home directories must have permissions of 750 or more restrictive (i.e., no group write, no "other" permissions). 5. Document the results for audit purposes.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure users own their home directories
description: The user home directory is space defined for the particular user to set local environment variables and to store personal files.
rationale: Since the user is accountable for files stored in the user home directory, the user must be the owner of the directory.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else owner=$(stat -L -c "%U" "$dir") if [ "$owner" != "$user" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user is owned by $owner." fi fi done
remediation: Change the ownership of any home directories that are not owned by the defined user to the correct user. Additional Information: On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires verification of OS-level file system ownership, which is not accessible via any GCP API, Google Cloud SDK, or GCP-native service. The check must be performed inside the guest OS (Container-Optimized OS) by running a script or command, as GCP does not expose per-user home directory ownership through its APIs or management tools.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Instance:** Use the Google Cloud Console or `gcloud` CLI to SSH into each relevant VM instance running Container-Optimized OS. Example: ```bash gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone=<ZONE> ``` 2. **Run the Audit Script:** Execute the following script on the instance to check home directory ownership: ```bash #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'$(which nologin)'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else owner=$(stat -L -c "%U" "$dir") if [ "$owner" != "$user" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user is owned by $owner." fi fi done ``` 3. **Review the Output:** - If no output is returned, all user home directories are correctly owned. - If output is returned, investigate and remediate as needed (e.g., using `chown` to correct ownership). 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** Each user's home directory must exist and be owned by that user. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** Rerun the script after remediation to ensure no findings are present.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure users' dot files are not group or world writable
description: While the system administrator can establish secure permissions for users' "dot" files, the users can easily override these.
rationale: Group or world-writable user configuration files may enable malicious users to steal or modify other users' data or to gain another user's system privileges.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else for file in $dir/.[A-Za-z0-9]*; do if [ ! -h "$file" -a -f "$file" ]; then fileperm=$(ls -ld $file | cut -f1 -d" ") if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c6)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group Write permission set on file $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c9)  != "-" ]; then echo "Other Write permission set on file $file" fi fi done fi done
remediation: Making global modifications to users' files without alerting the user community can result in unexpected outages and unhappy users. Therefore, it is recommended that a monitoring policy be established to report user dot file permissions and determine the action to be taken in accordance with site policy. Additional Information: On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires direct inspection of file permissions within the guest OS of each VM instance. GCP APIs, Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), and GCP CLI do not provide access to file-level permissions inside the operating system; they only manage infrastructure and metadata. No GCP-native service or API can enumerate or audit user dot file permissions within VM home directories.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into Each Relevant Compute Engine Instance:** Use the Google Cloud Console or `gcloud compute ssh` to access each Container Optimized OS instance where user home directories exist. 2. **Run the Provided Audit Script:** Execute the recommended shell script from the CIS benchmark to enumerate user dot files and check their permissions: ```bash grep -E -v '^(halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else for file in $dir/.[A-Za-z0-9]*; do if [ ! -h "$file" -a -f "$file" ]; then fileperm=$(ls -ld $file | cut -f1 -d" ") if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c6)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group writable: $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c9)  != "-" ]; then echo "World writable: $file" fi fi done fi done ``` 3. **Review Script Output:** Confirm that no dot files are reported as group or world writable. Any output indicates non-compliance. 4. **Document Results:** Record findings for each instance. If any non-compliant files are found, remediate according to site policy.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure no users have .forward files
description: The .forward file specifies an email address to forward the user's mail to.
rationale: Use of the .forward file poses a security risk in that sensitive data may be inadvertently transferred outside the organization. The .forward file also poses a risk as it can be used to execute commands that may perform unintended actions.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else if [ ! -h "$dir/.forward" -a -f "$dir/.forward" ]; then echo ".forward file $dir/.forward exists" fi fi done
remediation: Making global modifications to users' files without alerting the user community can result in unexpected outages and unhappy users. Therefore, it is recommended that a monitoring policy be established to report user .forward files and determine the action to be taken in accordance with site policy. Additional Information: On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that there is no GCP-native API, gcloud command, or management interface capable of inspecting user home directories or detecting `.forward` files within the guest OS of Container-Optimized OS (COS) instances. This check requires direct access to the VM's filesystem, which can only be accomplished via SSH or a remote execution mechanism (e.g., OS Config Run Command), both of which are operationally equivalent to manual inspection for compliance purposes.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each GCP VM instance running Container-Optimized OS. 2. Run the following script on each instance to check for `.forward` files in user home directories: ```bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'$(which nologin)'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else if [ ! -h "$dir/.forward" -a -f "$dir/.forward" ]; then echo ".forward file $dir/.forward exists" fi fi done ``` 3. Review the script output. If any `.forward` files are reported, this is non-compliant. 4. Confirm compliance by ensuring the script returns no results indicating `.forward` files for any user.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure no users have .netrc files
description: The .netrc file contains data for logging into a remote host for file transfers via FTP.
rationale: The .netrc file presents a significant security risk since it stores passwords in unencrypted form. Even if FTP is disabled, user accounts may have brought over .netrc files from other systems which could pose a risk to those systems.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else if [ ! -h "$dir/.netrc" -a -f "$dir/.netrc" ]; then echo ".netrc file $dir/.netrc exists" fi fi done
remediation: Making global modifications to users' files without alerting the user community can result in unexpected outages and unhappy users. Therefore, it is recommended that a monitoring policy be established to report user .netrc files and determine the action to be taken in accordance with site policy. Additional Information: On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires OS-level inspection of user home directories for the presence of `.netrc` files. There is no GCP-native API, gcloud command, or GCP Config/Cloud Asset Inventory capability that can enumerate or inspect files inside the guest OS of Compute Engine instances. This check inherently requires SSH access or similar direct interaction with the VM file system, which is outside the scope of GCP's API-driven automation.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each relevant Google Compute Engine instance running Container Optimized OS. 2. Enumerate all user accounts with valid shells (excluding system/service accounts) by running: ```bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "$(which nologin)" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' ``` 3. For each user and their home directory identified, check for the existence of a `.netrc` file: ```bash if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else if [ ! -h "$dir/.netrc" -a -f "$dir/.netrc" ]; then echo ".netrc file $dir/.netrc exists" fi fi ``` 4. Confirm compliance: No `.netrc` files should exist in any user home directory. If any are found, report and remediate per site policy.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure users' .netrc Files are not group or world accessible
description: While the system administrator can establish secure permissions for users' .netrc files, the users can easily override these.
rationale: .netrc files may contain unencrypted passwords that may be used to attack other systems.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else for file in $dir/.netrc; do if [ ! -h "$file" -a -f "$file" ]; then fileperm=$(ls -ld $file | cut -f1 -d" ") if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c5)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group Read set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c6)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group Write set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c7)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group Execute set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c8)  != "-" ]; then echo "Other Read set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c9)  != "-" ]; then echo "Other Write set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c10)  != "-" ]; then echo "Other Execute set on $file" fi fi done fi done
remediation: Making global modifications to users' files without alerting the user community can result in unexpected outages and unhappy users. Therefore, it is recommended that a monitoring policy be established to report user .netrc file permissions and determine the action to be taken in accordance with site policy. Additional Information: While the complete removal of .netrc files is recommended if any are required on the system secure permissions must be applied. On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires checking file permissions for `.netrc` files within user home directories on the OS filesystem of GCP Compute Engine instances. There are no GCP APIs, gcloud commands, or native GCP services that can directly enumerate or audit file permissions inside guest OS filesystems. The check must be performed from within the VM, either interactively or via a script executed on the instance. Therefore, automation via GCP APIs is not feasible.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Target Instance:** Use the GCP Console or gcloud CLI to SSH into each Compute Engine instance running Container Optimized OS that is in scope for the audit. 2. **Run the Audit Script:** Execute the following shell script on the instance to check for `.netrc` files with group or world access: ```bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'$(which nologin)'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else for file in $dir/.netrc; do if [ ! -h "$file" -a -f "$file" ]; then fileperm=$(ls -ld $file | cut -f1 -d" ") if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c5)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group Read permission set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c6)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group Write permission set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c7)  != "-" ]; then echo "Group Execute permission set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c8)  != "-" ]; then echo "Other Read permission set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c9)  != "-" ]; then echo "Other Write permission set on $file" fi if [ $(echo $fileperm | cut -c10)  != "-" ]; then echo "Other Execute permission set on $file" fi fi done fi done ``` 3. **Review Script Output:** Confirm that the script returns no output indicating group or world permissions on any `.netrc` file. Any findings must be remediated to restrict permissions (e.g., `chmod 600 ~/.netrc`). 4. **Document Results:** Record the results for compliance evidence, noting any instances where remediation was required.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure no users have .rhosts files
description: While no .rhosts files are shipped by default, users can easily create them.
rationale: This action is only meaningful if .rhosts support is permitted in the file /etc/pam.conf . Even though the .rhosts files are ineffective if support is disabled in /etc/pam.conf , they may have been brought over from other systems and could contain information useful to an attacker for those other systems.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'"$(which nologin)"'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else for file in $dir/.rhosts; do if [ ! -h "$file" -a -f "$file" ]; then echo ".rhosts file in $dir" fi done fi done
remediation: Making global modifications to users' files without alerting the user community can result in unexpected outages and unhappy users. Therefore, it is recommended that a monitoring policy be established to report user .rhosts files and determine the action to be taken in accordance with site policy. Additional Information: On some distributions the /sbin/nologin should be replaced with /usr/sbin/nologin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the presence of `.rhosts` files in user home directories on Container-Optimized OS instances cannot be determined via GCP APIs, gcloud, or any native GCP service. This check requires direct OS-level access to enumerate files within the guest filesystem, which is not exposed by GCP's control plane or management APIs. Therefore, the audit must be performed manually by executing a script on each instance.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into Each Instance:** Use SSH to log into every Google Container-Optimized OS VM instance that is in scope for the compliance check. 2. **Run the Audit Script:** Execute the following script on each instance to search for `.rhosts` files in user home directories: ```bash grep -E -v '^(root|halt|sync|shutdown)' /etc/passwd | awk -F: '($7 != "'$(which nologin)'" && $7 != "/bin/false") { print $1 " " $6 }' | while read user dir; do if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then echo "The home directory ($dir) of user $user does not exist." else for file in $dir/.rhosts; do if [ ! -h "$file" -a -f "$file" ]; then echo ".rhosts file in $dir" fi done fi done ``` 3. **Verify Output:** Confirm that the script returns no results indicating `.rhosts` files. Any output listing `.rhosts` files indicates non-compliance. 4. **Document Results:** Record the outcome for each instance as evidence of compliance or non-compliance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure all groups in /etc/passwd exist in /etc/group
description: Over time, system administration errors and changes can lead to groups being defined in /etc/passwd but not in /etc/group .
rationale: Groups defined in the /etc/passwd file but not in the /etc/group file pose a threat to system security since group permissions are not properly managed.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash for i in $(cut -s -d: -f4 /etc/passwd | sort -u ); do grep -q ":$i:" /etc/group if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then echo "Group $i is referenced by /etc/passwd but does not exist in /etc/group" fi done
remediation: Analyze the output of the Audit step above and perform the appropriate action to correct any discrepancies found.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires inspection of OS-level files (`/etc/passwd`, `/etc/group`) within the guest operating system of a Compute Engine instance running Container-Optimized OS. There is no GCP-native API, gcloud command, or GCP Config capability that can directly access or parse these files externally. While GCP's OS Config API can remotely execute scripts, it still operates inside the guest OS and does not expose this information to GCP APIs for direct compliance automation. Therefore, this control must be performed manually by accessing the instance.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Instance:** - Use the Google Cloud Console or CLI to SSH into each Container-Optimized OS instance. - Command: ``` gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone <ZONE_NAME> ``` 2. **Run the Audit Script:** - Execute the following script in the SSH session to identify any group IDs referenced in `/etc/passwd` that do not exist in `/etc/group`: ```bash for i in $(cut -s -d: -f4 /etc/passwd | sort -u ); do grep -q ":$i:" /etc/group if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then echo "Group $i is referenced by /etc/passwd but does not exist in /etc/group" fi done ``` 3. **Verify the Output:** - If the script returns no output, the control passes. - If any group IDs are listed, remediation is required to align `/etc/group` with `/etc/passwd`. 4. **Document Results:** - Record the outcome for each instance as evidence of compliance or non-compliance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure no duplicate UIDs exist
description: Although the useradd program will not let you create a duplicate User ID (UID), it is possible for an administrator to manually edit the /etc/passwd file and change the UID field.
rationale: Users must be assigned unique UIDs for accountability and to ensure appropriate access protections.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash cut -f3 -d":" /etc/passwd | sort -n | uniq -c | while read x ; do [ -z "$x" ] && break set - $x if [ $1 -gt 1 ]; then users=$(awk -F: '($3 == n) { print $1 }' n=$2 /etc/passwd | xargs) echo "Duplicate UID ($2): $users" fi done
remediation: Based on the results of the audit script, establish unique UIDs and review all files owned by the shared UIDs to determine which UID they are supposed to belong to.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that checking for duplicate UIDs in `/etc/passwd` on Container-Optimized OS instances requires direct access to the guest OS, which is not exposed via any GCP API, gcloud CLI, or GCP-native automation tools. GCP does not provide a mechanism to inspect or manage OS-level user accounts remotely through its APIs; this check must be performed manually on the instance itself.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the Instance:** - SSH into the target Google Cloud VM instance running Container-Optimized OS using: ``` gcloud compute ssh [INSTANCE_NAME] --zone [ZONE] ``` 2. **Run the Audit Script:** - Execute the following shell script to check for duplicate UIDs: ```bash cut -f3 -d":" /etc/passwd | sort -n | uniq -c | while read x ; do [ -z "$x" ] && break set - $x if [ $1 -gt 1 ]; then users=$(awk -F: '($3 == n) { print $1 }' n=$2 /etc/passwd | xargs) echo "Duplicate UID ($2): $users" fi done ``` 3. **Verify Output:** - Confirm that the script returns no output. Any output indicates duplicate UIDs, which is non-compliant. 4. **Remediation (if needed):** - If duplicates are found, manually edit `/etc/passwd` to assign unique UIDs and review file ownership as per the control’s remediation guidance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure no duplicate user names exist
description: Although the useradd program will not let you create a duplicate user name, it is possible for an administrator to manually edit the /etc/passwd file and change the user name.
rationale: If a user is assigned a duplicate user name, it will create and have access to files with the first UID for that username in /etc/passwd . For example, if "test4" has a UID of 1000 and a subsequent "test4" entry has a UID of 2000, logging in as "test4" will use UID 1000. Effectively, the UID is shared, which is a security problem.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash cut -f1 -d":" /etc/passwd | sort -n | uniq -c | while read x ; do [ -z "$x" ] && break set - $x if [ $1 -gt 1 ]; then uids=$(awk -F: '($1 == n) { print $3 }' n=$2 /etc/passwd | xargs) echo "Duplicate User Name ($2): $uids" fi done
remediation: Based on the results of the audit script, establish unique user names for the users. File ownerships will automatically reflect the change as long as the users have unique UIDs.
justification: This control requires checking for duplicate user names in the `/etc/passwd` file on GCP Compute Engine instances running Container-Optimized OS. There is no GCP API, gcloud command, or native GCP service that exposes or audits OS-level user account data directly. While the check itself can be scripted at the OS level, GCP does not provide a managed, agentless, or API-based method to enumerate or validate user names in `/etc/passwd` across instances. Therefore, this must be performed manually via SSH or using a script executed directly on the instance.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Target Instance:** - Use the GCP Console or `gcloud compute ssh` to connect to the instance running Container-Optimized OS. 2. **Run the Audit Script:** - Execute the following script to check for duplicate user names: ```bash cut -f1 -d":" /etc/passwd | sort -n | uniq -c | while read x ; do [ -z "$x" ] && break set - $x if [ $1 -gt 1 ]; then uids=$(awk -F: '($1 == n) { print $3 }' n=$2 /etc/passwd | xargs) echo "Duplicate User Name ($2): $uids" fi done ``` 3. **Review the Output:** - Ensure that no output is returned. Any output indicates duplicate user names, which is non-compliant. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - Each user name in `/etc/passwd` must be unique. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Compliance is confirmed if the script returns no results.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure no duplicate group names exist
description: Although the groupadd program will not let you create a duplicate group name, it is possible for an administrator to manually edit the /etc/group file and change the group name.
rationale: If a group is assigned a duplicate group name, it will create and have access to files with the first GID for that group in /etc/group . Effectively, the GID is shared, which is a security problem.
audit: Run the following script and verify no results are returned: #!/bin/bash cut -f1 -d":" /etc/group | sort -n | uniq -c | while read x ; do [ -z "$x" ] && break set - $x if [ $1 -gt 1 ]; then gids=$(gawk -F: '($1 == n) { print $3 }' n=$2 /etc/group | xargs) echo "Duplicate Group Name ($2): $gids" fi done
remediation: Based on the results of the audit script, establish unique names for the user groups. File group ownerships will automatically reflect the change as long as the groups have unique GIDs.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires checking for duplicate group names within the `/etc/group` file on Google Container-Optimized OS (COS) instances. There is no GCP API, gcloud command, or native GCP service that can directly access or enumerate the contents of `/etc/group` on a running VM without executing a script inside the guest OS. This check inherently requires manual or script-based inspection at the OS level, which is outside the scope of GCP-native automation.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target Container-Optimized OS (COS) instance using the GCP Console or your terminal. 2. Copy and execute the following shell script on the instance: ```bash cut -f1 -d":" /etc/group | sort -n | uniq -c | while read x ; do [ -z "$x" ] && break set - $x if [ $1 -gt 1 ]; then gids=$(gawk -F: '($1 == n) { print $3 }' n=$2 /etc/group | xargs) echo "Duplicate Group Name ($2): $gids" fi done ``` 3. Review the script output. If no output is returned, all group names are unique and the control passes. 4. If duplicate group names are reported, edit `/etc/group` to ensure all group names are unique. Confirm that file group ownerships remain correct and unique by GID.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure shadow group is empty
description: The shadow group allows system programs which require access the ability to read the /etc/shadow file. No users should be assigned to the shadow group.
rationale: Any users assigned to the shadow group would be granted read access to the /etc/shadow file. If attackers can gain read access to the /etc/shadow file, they can easily run a password cracking program against the hashed passwords to break them. Other security information that is stored in the /etc/shadow file (such as expiration) could also be useful to subvert additional user accounts.
audit: Run the following commands and verify no results are returned: # grep ^shadow:[^:]*:[^:]*:[^:]+ /etc/group # awk -F: '($4 == "<shadow-gid>") { print }' /etc/passwd
remediation: Remove all users from the shadow group, and change the primary group of any users with shadow as their primary group.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that this control requires inspection of OS-level files (`/etc/group` and `/etc/passwd`) inside the VM instance, which are not accessible via any GCP API, gcloud CLI, or GCP-native automation tools. GCP does not provide an API or service to directly query or audit the membership of the `shadow` group within the guest OS. Therefore, this check must be performed manually by accessing the instance.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the Target Instance:** SSH into the Container-Optimized OS instance that needs to be audited. 2. **Check for Non-Empty Shadow Group:** Run the following command to check if any users are assigned to the shadow group: ```bash sudo grep ^shadow:[^:]*:[^:]*:[^:]+ /etc/group ``` - **What to verify:** No output should be returned. If there is output, users are assigned to the shadow group, which is non-compliant. 3. **Identify Users with Shadow as Primary Group:** First, determine the GID of the shadow group: ```bash grep ^shadow: /etc/group ``` Note the GID (third field). Then, run: ```bash sudo awk -F: -v gid=<shadow-gid> '($4 == gid) { print }' /etc/passwd ``` Replace `<shadow-gid>` with the actual GID from the previous command. - **What to verify:** No output should be returned. If there is output, those users have shadow as their primary group, which is non-compliant. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** Both commands should return no results, indicating the shadow group is empty and no user has it as their primary group. 5. **How to Confirm Compliance:** If both checks return no output, the instance is compliant. If any output is returned, remediation is required to remove users from the shadow group.

source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
description: If kube-proxy is running, and if it is configured by a kubeconfig file, ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
rationale: The kube-proxy kubeconfig file controls various parameters of the kube-proxy service on the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable only by the administrators on the system. Impact: Overly permissive file permissions increase security risk to the platform.
audit: Using Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. Click on the desired cluster to open the Details page, then click on the desired Node pool to open the Node pool Details page 3. Note the name of the desired node 4. Go to VM Instances by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances 5. Find the desired node and click on 'SSH' to open an SSH connection to the node. Using Command Line Method 1 SSH to the worker nodes To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate kubeconfig file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --kubeconfig /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig which is the location of the kubeconfig file. Run this command to obtain the kubeconfig file permissions: stat -c %a /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig The output of the above command gives you the kubeconfig file's permissions. Verify that if a file is specified and it exists, the permissions are 644 or more restrictive. Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file permissions on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the permission level of the file: ls -l /host/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig Verify that if a file is specified and it exists, the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 644 <proxy kubeconfig file> Default Value: The default permissions of the proxy kubeconfig file are 644. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/ 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cis-benchmarks
impact: Overly permissive file permissions increase security risk to the platform.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires manual verification because GCP APIs, the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), and GCP CLI do not provide access to the underlying file system of GKE worker nodes. File permissions for the kube-proxy kubeconfig file are only accessible via SSH or by running a privileged pod on each node. There is no GCP-native method to automate this check.
manual_summary: 1. In the Google Cloud Console, navigate to Kubernetes Engine > Clusters. 2. Select the desired cluster, then the appropriate node pool, and identify the node(s) to check. 3. Go to Compute Engine > VM Instances, locate the node(s), and open an SSH session to each. 4. On each node, locate the kube-proxy kubeconfig file (commonly at `/var/lib/kube-proxy/kubeconfig` or as specified by the kube-proxy process). 5. Run `stat -c %a <proxy kubeconfig file>` to check the file permissions. 6. Confirm that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive (i.e., 600, 640, or 644). 7. Document the results for each node. 8. If permissions are too permissive, remediate by running `chmod 644 <proxy kubeconfig file>`.

source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file ownership is set to root:root
description: If kube-proxy is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root.
rationale: The kubeconfig file for kube-proxy controls various parameters for the kube-proxy service in the worker node. You should set its file ownership to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root. Impact: Overly permissive file access increases the security risk to the platform.
audit: Using Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. Click on the desired cluster to open the Details page, then click on the desired Node pool to open the Node pool Details page 3. Note the name of the desired node 4. Go to VM Instances by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances 5. Find the desired node and click on 'SSH' to open an SSH connection to the node. Using Command Line Method 1 SSH to the worker nodes To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate kubeconfig file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --kubeconfig /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig which is the location of the kubeconfig file. Run this command to obtain the kubeconfig file ownership: stat -c %U:%G /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig The output of the above command gives you the kubeconfig file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root. Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file ownership on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the ownership of the file: ls -l /host/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig The output of the above command gives you the kubeconfig file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on each worker node. For example, chown root:root <proxy kubeconfig file> Default Value: The default ownership of the proxy kubeconfig file is root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/ 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cis-benchmarks
impact: Overly permissive file access increases the security risk to the platform.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the ownership of the kube-proxy kubeconfig file on GKE worker nodes requires OS-level access, which is not exposed via any GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native automation tool. The audit steps provided in the control explicitly require SSH access to the node, and there is no GCP-native method to retrieve file ownership details from within the managed GKE environment.
manual_summary: 1. In the Google Cloud Console, navigate to Kubernetes Engine (https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list) and select the desired cluster and node pool. 2. Identify the specific node to be checked, then go to Compute Engine > VM Instances (https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances) and open an SSH session to the node. 3. Once connected via SSH, locate the kube-proxy kubeconfig file (commonly at `/var/lib/kube-proxy/kubeconfig` or as specified by the kube-proxy process). 4. Run the following command to verify file ownership: ``` stat -c %U:%G <proxy kubeconfig file> ``` The output must be `root:root`. 5. Repeat for each worker node in the cluster. 6. If the ownership is not `root:root`, remediate by running: ``` sudo chown root:root <proxy kubeconfig file> ``` 7. Document the verification and remediation steps for audit purposes.

Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive. Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file permissions on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the permission level of the file: ls -l /host/etc/kubernetes/kubelet-config.yaml Verify that if a file is specified and it exists, the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the following command (using the kubelet config file location): chmod 644 <kubelet_config_file> Default Value: The default permissions for the kubelet configuration file are 600. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/kubelet-config-file/ 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cis-benchmarks
impact: Overly permissive file access increases the security risk to the platform.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that there is no GCP API, gcloud command, or native GCP service that can directly inspect file permissions on the kubelet configuration file within GKE worker nodes. This check requires OS-level access—either via SSH or a privileged pod—to view file permissions, which is not exposed through any GCP-managed interface or API. Therefore, this control cannot be automated using GCP-native tooling.
manual_summary: 1. **Identify the Node:** - In the Google Cloud Console, navigate to Kubernetes Engine > Clusters. - Select the relevant cluster, then the desired node pool, and note the node name. 2. **Access the Node:** - Go to Compute Engine > VM Instances. - Locate the node VM and click 'SSH' to open a terminal session. 3. **Locate the Kubelet Config File:** - Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to find the `--config` argument, which shows the kubelet config file path (commonly `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`). 4. **Check File Permissions:** - Run `ls -l <kubelet_config_file>` (replace with the actual path found above). - Confirm the permissions are `-rw-r--r--` (644). 5. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The file should have permissions set to 644 (owner read/write, group and others read-only). 6. **Confirm Compliance:** - If the permissions are not 644, the node is non-compliant.

source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure that the kubelet configuration file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure that if the kubelet configuration file exists, it is owned by root:root.
rationale: The kubelet reads various parameters, including security settings, from a config file specified by the --config argument. If this file is specified you should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root. Impact: Overly permissive file access increases the security risk to the platform.
audit: Using Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. Click on the desired cluster to open the Details page, then click on the desired Node pool to open the Node pool Details page 3. Note the name of the desired node 4. Go to VM Instances by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances 5. Find the desired node and click on 'SSH' to open an SSH connection to the node. Using Command Line Method 1 First, SSH to the relevant worker node: To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate Kubelet config file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --config /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.yaml which is the location of the Kubelet config file. Run the following command: stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.yaml The output of the above command is the Kubelet config file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file ownership on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the ownership of the file: ls -l /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.yaml The output of the above command gives you the file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the following command (using the config file location identified in the Audit step): chown root:root <kubelet_config_file> Default Value: The default file ownership is root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/kubelet-config-file/ 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cis-benchmarks
impact: Overly permissive file access increases the security risk to the platform.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the ownership of the kubelet configuration file on GKE nodes requires direct access to the underlying VM's filesystem, which is not exposed via any GCP API, Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), or GCP-native automation tools. GCP does not provide an API or CLI method to inspect OS-level file ownership or permissions on managed Kubernetes nodes. Therefore, this control can only be audited through manual SSH access or by running a privileged pod with hostPath access.
manual_summary: 1. In the Google Cloud Console, navigate to Kubernetes Engine → Clusters. Select the target cluster, then the relevant node pool, and note the node name. 2. Go to Compute Engine → VM Instances. Locate the VM instance corresponding to the node and click 'SSH' to open a terminal session. 3. Determine the kubelet configuration file path by running: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` Look for the `--config` flag to identify the config file location (commonly `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.yaml`). 4. Check the file ownership by running: `stat -c %U:%G <kubelet_config_file>` or `ls -l <kubelet_config_file>` 5. Confirm that the output is `root:root`. If not, this is non-compliant. 6. Document the findings for compliance records.

source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure that the Anonymous Auth is Not Enabled Draft
description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
rationale: When enabled, requests that are not rejected by other configured authentication methods are treated as anonymous requests. These requests are then served by the Kubelet server. You should rely on authentication to authorize access and disallow anonymous requests. Impact: Anonymous requests will be rejected.
audit: Audit Method 1: Kubelets can accept configuration via a configuration file and in some cases via command line arguments. It is important to note that parameters provided as command line arguments will override their counterpart parameters in the configuration file (see -- config details in the Kubelet CLI Reference for more info, where you can also find out which configuration parameters can be supplied as a command line argument). With this in mind, it is important to check for the existence of command line arguments as well as configuration file entries when auditing Kubelet configuration. Firstly, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the Kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active Kubelet process, from which we can see the command line arguments provided to the process. Also note the location of the configuration file, provided with the --config argument, as this will be needed to verify configuration. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Verify that Anonymous Authentication is not enabled. This may be configured as a command line argument to the kubelet service with --anonymous-auth=false or in the kubelet configuration file via "authentication": { "anonymous": { "enabled": false }. Audit Method 2: It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint of the Kubernetes API. This can be achieved using kubectl to proxy your requests to the API. Discover all nodes in your cluster by running the following command: kubectl get nodes Next, initiate a proxy with kubectl on a local port of your choice. In this example we will use 8080: kubectl proxy --port=8080 With this running, in a separate terminal run the following command for each node: export NODE_NAME=my-node-name curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz The curl command will return the API response which will be a JSON formatted string representing the Kubelet configuration. Verify that Anonymous Authentication is not enabled checking that "authentication": { "anonymous": { "enabled": false } is in the API response.
remediation: Remediation Method 1: If configuring via the Kubelet config file, you first need to locate the file. To do this, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active kubelet process, from which we can see the location of the configuration file provided to the kubelet service with the --config argument. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Disable Anonymous Authentication by setting the following parameter: "authentication": { "anonymous": { "enabled": false } } Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file on each worker node and ensure the below parameters are part of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. For systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, then this file can be found at /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf. Otherwise, you may need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: --anonymous-auth=false For Both Remediation Steps: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check the service status. The following example is for operating systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, and invokes the systemctl command. If systemctl is not available then you will need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l Default Value: See the GKE documentation for the default value. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/kubelet-authn- authz/#kubelet-authentication 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/config-api/kubelet-config.v1beta1/
impact: Anonymous requests will be rejected.
justification: Direct verification of the Kubelet's anonymous authentication setting requires access to node-level configuration files or process arguments, which are not exposed via GCP APIs, Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), or any GKE-native API. While partial automation via the Kubernetes API (`/configz` endpoint) is theoretically possible, it is not universally reliable or supported across all GKE cluster types and may be restricted by network policies or cluster configuration. Therefore, the only consistently valid method is manual inspection via SSH.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH to Each Node:** Use the GCP Console or `gcloud compute ssh` to connect to each node in your GKE cluster. 2. **Identify the Kubelet Process:** Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to locate the Kubelet process and note its command-line arguments and the path to its configuration file (look for the `--config` flag). 3. **Inspect the Kubelet Configuration File:** Use `sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.yaml` (or `.json`) to open the configuration file. - Confirm that the file contains: `"authentication": { "anonymous": { "enabled": false } }` 4. **Check Command-Line Arguments:** Ensure the Kubelet process includes `--anonymous-auth=false` if the setting is managed via command-line arguments. 5. **Document Findings:** Record the configuration and command-line settings for audit evidence.

source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure that the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow
description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
rationale: Kubelets can be configured to allow all authenticated requests (even anonymous ones) without needing explicit authorization checks from the apiserver. You should restrict this behavior and only allow explicitly authorized requests. Impact: Unauthorized requests will be denied.
audit: Audit Method 1: Kubelets can accept configuration via a configuration file and in some cases via command line arguments. It is important to note that parameters provided as command line arguments will override their counterpart parameters in the configuration file (see -- config details in the Kubelet CLI Reference for more info, where you can also find out which configuration parameters can be supplied as a command line argument). With this in mind, it is important to check for the existence of command line arguments as well as configuration file entries when auditing Kubelet configuration. Firstly, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the Kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active Kubelet process, from which we can see the command line arguments provided to the process. Also note the location of the configuration file, provided with the --config argument, as this will be needed to verify configuration. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Verify that Webhook Authentication is enabled. This may be enabled as a command line argument to the kubelet service with --authentication-token-webhook or in the kubelet configuration file via "authentication": { "webhook": { "enabled": true } }. Verify that the Authorization Mode is set to WebHook. This may be set as a command line argument to the kubelet service with --authorization-mode=Webhook or in the configuration file via "authorization": { "mode": "Webhook }. Audit Method 2: It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint of the Kubernetes API. This can be achieved using kubectl to proxy your requests to the API. Discover all nodes in your cluster by running the following command: kubectl get nodes Next, initiate a proxy with kubectl on a local port of your choice. In this example we will use 8080: kubectl proxy --port=8080 With this running, in a separate terminal run the following command for each node: export NODE_NAME=my-node-name curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz The curl command will return the API response which will be a JSON formatted string representing the Kubelet configuration. Verify that Webhook Authentication is enabled with "authentication": { "webhook": { "enabled": true } } in the API response. Verify that the Authorization Mode is set to WebHook with "authorization": { "mode": "Webhook } in the API response.
remediation: Remediation Method 1: If configuring via the Kubelet config file, you first need to locate the file. To do this, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active kubelet process, from which we can see the location of the configuration file provided to the kubelet service with the --config argument. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Enable Webhook Authentication by setting the following parameter: "authentication": { "webhook": { "enabled": true } } Next, set the Authorization Mode to Webhook by setting the following parameter: "authorization": { "mode": "Webhook } Finer detail of the authentication and authorization fields can be found in the Kubelet Configuration documentation. Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file on each worker node and ensure the below parameters are part of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. For systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, then this file can be found at /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf. Otherwise, you may need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: --authentication-token-webhook --authorization-mode=Webhook For Both Remediation Steps: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check the service status. The following example is for operating systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, and invokes the systemctl command. If systemctl is not available then you will need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l Default Value: See the GKE documentation for the default value. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/kubelet-authn- authz/#kubelet-authentication 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/config-api/kubelet-config.v1beta1/
impact: Unauthorized requests will be denied.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that GCP does not expose kubelet process arguments or configuration files via any API, gcloud command, or GCP-native automation. The only way to verify the `--authorization-mode` flag is by directly accessing each node via SSH and inspecting the running kubelet process or its configuration file. This is a node-level setting not surfaced through GKE APIs or management interfaces.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each node in the GKE cluster using the appropriate credentials and permissions. 2. Run the command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to identify the running kubelet process and its command-line arguments. 3. Examine the process arguments to ensure that `--authorization-mode` is NOT set to `AlwaysAllow`. 4. If the kubelet uses a configuration file (check for the `--config` flag in the process), note the file path. 5. Open the configuration file (e.g., `sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.yaml`) and verify that the `authorization.mode` field is NOT set to `AlwaysAllow`. 6. Document your findings for each node. 7. Compliance is confirmed if no kubelet process or configuration file on any node sets `authorization-mode` to `AlwaysAllow`.

source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure that a Client CA File is Configured
description: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
rationale: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests. Impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
audit: Audit Method 1: Kubelets can accept configuration via a configuration file and in some cases via command line arguments. It is important to note that parameters provided as command line arguments will override their counterpart parameters in the configuration file (see -- config details in the Kubelet CLI Reference for more info, where you can also find out which configuration parameters can be supplied as a command line argument). With this in mind, it is important to check for the existence of command line arguments as well as configuration file entries when auditing Kubelet configuration. Firstly, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the Kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active Kubelet process, from which we can see the command line arguments provided to the process. Also note the location of the configuration file, provided with the --config argument, as this will be needed to verify configuration. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Verify that a client certificate authority file is configured. This may be configured using a command line argument to the kubelet service with --client-ca-file or in the kubelet configuration file via "authentication": { "x509": {"clientCAFile": <path/to/client-ca-file> } }". Audit Method 2: It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint of the Kubernetes API. This can be achieved using kubectl to proxy your requests to the API. Discover all nodes in your cluster by running the following command: kubectl get nodes Next, initiate a proxy with kubectl on a local port of your choice. In this example we will use 8080: kubectl proxy --port=8080 With this running, in a separate terminal run the following command for each node: export NODE_NAME=my-node-name curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz The curl command will return the API response which will be a JSON formatted string representing the Kubelet configuration. Verify that a client certificate authority file is configured with "authentication": { "x509": {"clientCAFile": <path/to/client-ca-file> } }" in the API response.
remediation: Remediation Method 1: If configuring via the Kubelet config file, you first need to locate the file. To do this, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active kubelet process, from which we can see the location of the configuration file provided to the kubelet service with the --config argument. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Configure the client certificate authority file by setting the following parameter appropriately: "authentication": { "x509": {"clientCAFile": <path/to/client-ca-file> } }" Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file on each worker node and ensure the below parameters are part of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. For systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, then this file can be found at /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf. Otherwise, you may need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: --client-ca-file=<path/to/client-ca-file> For Both Remediation Steps: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check the service status. The following example is for operating systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, and invokes the systemctl command. If systemctl is not available then you will need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l Default Value: See the GKE documentation for the default value. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/kubelet-authn- authz/#kubelet-authentication 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/config-api/kubelet-config.v1beta1/
impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that GCP APIs, gcloud CLI, and GCP-native tools do not expose Kubelet process arguments or configuration files required to verify the presence of a client CA file. The only feasible methods involve SSH access to nodes or using the Kubernetes API with a proxy and elevated permissions, neither of which are GCP-native or fully automatable at scale. Therefore, this control requires manual verification.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH to Each Node:** - Use `gcloud compute ssh [NODE_NAME]` to access each node in the GKE cluster. 2. **Identify Kubelet Process:** - Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to locate the kubelet process and review its command-line arguments. - Look specifically for the `--client-ca-file` parameter. 3. **Check Kubelet Config File:** - If the kubelet is started with a `--config` argument, note the file path. - View the configuration file using `sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json`. - Verify that the configuration contains `"authentication": { "x509": {"clientCAFile": "<path/to/client-ca-file>" }}`. 4. **(Optional) Use Kubernetes API:** - If cluster-admin access is available, start a proxy with `kubectl proxy --port=8080`. - Access the configz endpoint: `curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/[NODE_NAME]/proxy/configz`. - Review the output for the `clientCAFile` setting. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Compliance is confirmed if every node’s kubelet process is configured with a valid client CA file, either via command-line or configuration file.

source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure that the --read-only-port is disabled
description: Disable the read-only port.
rationale: The Kubelet process provides a read-only API in addition to the main Kubelet API. Unauthenticated access is provided to this read-only API which could possibly retrieve potentially sensitive information about the cluster. Impact: Removal of the read-only port will require that any service which made use of it will need to be re-configured to use the main Kubelet API.
audit: If using a Kubelet configuration file, check that there is an entry for authentication: anonymous: enabled set to 0. First, SSH to the relevant node: Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate Kubelet config file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --config /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json which is the location of the Kubelet config file. Open the Kubelet config file: cat /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json Verify that the --read-only-port argument exists and is set to 0. If the --read-only-port argument is not present, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config. Check that if there is a readOnlyPort entry in the file, it is set to 0.
remediation: If modifying the Kubelet config file, edit the kubelet-config.json file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json and set the below parameter to 0 "readOnlyPort": 0 If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf on each worker node and add the below parameter at the end of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. --read-only-port=0 For each remediation: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check status systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l Default Value: See the GKE documentation for the default value. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/
impact: Removal of the read-only port will require that any service which made use of it will need to be re-configured to use the main Kubelet API.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the Kubelet `--read-only-port` setting requires direct inspection of node-level configuration files or process arguments, which are not exposed via any GCP API, Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`), or GCP Console. GKE does not provide a managed or API-accessible method to retrieve Kubelet flags or configuration for individual nodes. Therefore, SSH access and manual inspection are the only viable audit methods.
